text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: filter keeps even numbers (2, 4). map multiplies by 10 giving 20, 40. forEach prints each on a new line.

text: Which of the following are terminal stream operations? (Choose all that apply.)
explanation: Terminal operations consume the stream and produce a result or side effect: collect(), reduce(), count(), findFirst(), forEach(), anyMatch(), etc. filter() and map() are intermediate operations.

text: What does the following code produce?
explanation: No element is greater than 10, so the stream is empty after filter. findFirst() returns an empty OptionalInt. isPresent() returns false.

text: Which collector groups a stream of strings by their length?
explanation: groupingBy(String::length) creates a Map<Integer, List<String>> keyed by string length. partitioningBy requires a predicate returning boolean. toMap would fail on duplicate keys. mapping transforms elements before collecting.

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: Streams are lazy and process one element at a time through the pipeline. For 'a': peek1(a1), filter(pass), peek2(a2). For 'b': peek1(b1), filter(reject, pipeline stops). For 'c': peek1(c1), filter(pass), peek2(c2). Output: a1 a2 b1 c1 c2.

text: What is the result of Stream.of(1, 2, 3).reduce(0, Integer::sum)?
explanation: reduce(identity, accumulator) returns the result type directly (not Optional) because the identity value ensures a result is always present. With identity 0 and Integer::sum, the result is 0+1+2+3 = 6.

text: Which of the following Stream operations are stateful intermediate operations? (Choose all that apply.)
explanation: Stateful intermediate operations must see multiple elements to produce results: sorted() needs all elements, distinct() tracks seen values, limit() tracks count. filter() and map() are stateless — they process each element independently.

text: What does the following code print?
explanation: Gatherers.windowFixed(3) groups stream elements into non-overlapping windows of exactly 3. Stream of 6 elements produces two windows: [1,2,3] and [4,5,6]. The result is a List<List<Integer>>.
source: JEP 485 – Stream Gatherers (finalized in Java 25): https://openjdk.org/jeps/485

text: Which of the following Gatherers are available in the standard java.util.stream.Gatherers class? (Choose all that apply.)
explanation: The standard Gatherers class (JEP 485) provides: windowFixed, windowSliding, scan, fold, and mapConcurrent. There is no Gatherers.batch() — use windowFixed for fixed-size batching.
source: JEP 485 – Stream Gatherers (finalized in Java 25): https://openjdk.org/jeps/485 | Java SE 25 API Docs: https://docs.oracle.com/en/java/javase/25/docs/api/java.base/java/util/stream/Gatherers.html

text: What does the following code print?

options.text: Stream output is non-deterministic — order is not guaranteed
Compiler error on the flatMap line

explanation: map(list -> list.stream()) produces a Stream<Stream<Integer>>; iterating with forEach and nested forEach still prints each integer in encounter order. flatMap(list -> list.stream()) flattens the nested streams into a single Stream<Integer> and also prints 12345. Both lines yield the same output because the source is an ordered list. The key difference is the intermediate type: map gives Stream<Stream<Integer>> (requiring double iteration) while flatMap gives Stream<Integer> directly.

text: Given the following code, which statement about orElse and orElseGet is correct?

options.text: Both lines call expensiveDefault() exactly once.
Line A always calls expensiveDefault() regardless of whether the Optional is empty; line B calls expensiveDefault() only when the Optional is empty.
Line B always calls expensiveDefault() regardless of whether the Optional is empty; line A calls expensiveDefault() only when the Optional is empty.
orElse and orElseGet behave identically — the difference is only syntactic.

explanation: orElse(T other) is an eager method: the argument expression is evaluated before the method is called, so expensiveDefault() runs unconditionally even if the Optional contains a value. orElseGet(Supplier<T>) is lazy: the Supplier is only invoked when the Optional is empty. Prefer orElseGet for expensive computations or side-effect-bearing suppliers.

text: What is the type and value of result after this code executes?

options.text: Map<Boolean, List<String>> with keys true and false; true -> [elephant, hippopotamus], false -> [cat, ox, bee]
Map<Boolean, List<String>> with only key true because no element fails the predicate
Map<Integer, List<String>> keyed by string length

explanation: partitioningBy always produces exactly two keys — true and false — regardless of whether any element matches. Elements with length > 3 ('elephant', 'hippopotamus') go to true; 'cat' (3), 'ox' (2), 'bee' (3) go to false. This is its key guarantee over groupingBy: both partitions are always present in the result map.

text: Consider the following code. Which statements are true about the two collectors used? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: The downstream collector Collectors.counting() counts the elements in each group, producing Map<Integer, Long>.
Without a downstream collector, groupingBy would produce Map<Integer, List<String>> by default.
The output is {2=1, 5=2, 4=2} because 'hi' has length 2, 'hello'/'world' have length 5, and 'hey'/'word' have length 4.
Collectors.counting() returns a Collector<T, ?, Integer>, so the map value type is Integer, not Long.
A downstream collector can itself be another groupingBy, allowing multi-level grouping.

explanation: A: correct — counting() accumulates the size of each downstream group as Long. B: correct — the single-argument groupingBy defaults to Collectors.toList() as the downstream collector. C: correct — lengths are 2 (hi), 5 (hello, world), 4 (hey, word). D: incorrect — Collectors.counting() returns Collector<T, ?, Long>, producing Long values. E: correct — groupingBy can be nested, e.g., groupingBy(keyFn, groupingBy(secondaryFn)).

text: What does the following code print?

options.text: Compiler error — Stream.iterate does not accept a predicate

explanation: The two-argument iterate(seed, unaryOp) produces an infinite stream; limit(5) cuts it to 1, 2, 4, 8, 16. The three-argument iterate(seed, hasNext, unaryOp) introduced in Java 9 stops when the predicate returns false: starting at 1, doubling each time — 1 (< 20 true), 2 (true), 4 (true), 8 (true), 16 (true), next would be 32 but 32 < 20 is false so the stream ends. Output of the second line is 1 2 4 8 16, but 16 IS included because the predicate is checked before producing the next element (16 itself is produced because 16 < 20 is true; the stream stops before producing 32).

text: What does the following code print?
explanation: takeWhile stops at the first element that does NOT satisfy the predicate. 1, 2, 3 satisfy n < 4; 4 does not, so the stream ends there — elements after 4 (including 1, 2 at the end) are never seen. dropWhile discards elements as long as the predicate holds: drops 1, 2, 3, then yields 4, 5, 1, 2. Crucially, once 4 fails the predicate, dropWhile stops dropping and passes all remaining elements through — including 1 and 2 at the end, even though they would satisfy the predicate. Both operations are defined on ordered streams and rely on encounter order; behaviour on unordered streams is non-deterministic.
